retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, High Density Lipoprotein -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated HDL levels are associated with a decreased risk of coronary heart disease and are thought to play a role in predicting cardiovascular risk. However, cholesterol ester transfer protein deficiency, primary familial hyperalphalipoproteinemia, and endothelial lipase deficiency result in extremely high levels of HDL, which may mitigate the protective effects and even, paradoxically, increase cardiovascular risk. [15] Additionally, there are secondary causes that can cause high levels of HDL to accumulate, such as primary biliary cirrhosis and thyroid disorders. [16] [17] HDL levels can also increase with lifestyle changes, such as smoking cessation, diet, and exercise. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MΑSLD) -- Evaluation -- Assessment of comorbidities
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular disease evaluation incorporates fasting plasma uric acid, serum high-sensitivity C-reactive protein, serum ferritin, and blood pressure measurements, with optional 24-hour ambulatory blood pressure monitoring, echocardiography for heart failure assessment, serum NT-ProBNP, and transferrin saturation analysis. Atherosclerosis assessment requires complete blood count with platelet evaluation, recognizing that elevated lipoprotein-a represents an independent causal risk factor for atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Supplementary investigations include fibrinogen, homocysteine, von Willebrand factor antigen, carotid artery intima-media thickness, echo-Doppler plaque instability assessment, and coronary artery calcification scoring.

retrieved_docs.title: Left Ventricular Hypertrophy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: ECG is relatively insensitive in diagnosing LVH because it relies on the measurement of the electrical activity of the heart by electrodes placed on the surface of the skin to predict the left ventricular mass. The intracardiac electrical signal is problematic to measure in this way because the measurements are impacted by all elements that lie between the heart muscle and the ECG electrodes, specifically fat, fluid, and air. Because of the variations in these elements, ECG underdiagnoses LVH in patients with pleural effusions, pericardial effusions, anasarca, obesity as well as chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). Also, LVH diagnosed by ECG is strongly impacted by both age and ethnicity. While electrocardiography is not sensitive and cannot be used to definitively exclude the diagnosis of LVH, it still plays a diagnostic and management role. In the Losartan Intervention for Endpoint Reduction in Hypertension (LIFE) study, LVH regression (diagnosed by ECG utilizing the Sokolow-Lyon index or the Cornell product criteria) in response to losartan (Cozaar) improved clinical cardiovascular outcomes independent of blood pressure response.

retrieved_docs.title: Atherosclerosis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: In the assessment of ASCVD risk factors, measuring the lipid profile (LDL-cholesterol), plasma glucose, and high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (hsCRP) (in certain instances) is reasonable. Ultrasound of the abdomen to screen for an abdominal aneurysm is indicated in the elderly especially with other ASCVD risk factors. In screening for peripheral artery disease (PAD), a Doppler device is used to obtain the ankle-brachial index (normal 1.0 to 1.40) and is a very useful, cost-effective test to rule out PAD. It needs to be emphasized that PAD is a marker for ASCVD in other beds (coronary artery disease [CAD], cerebrovascular disease, among others). Also, sonography of the carotids to rule out stenosis if a patient has a carotid bruit. An electrocardiogram (ECG) may be useful if indicated, for example, stress ECG. To confirm ASCVD, a reasonable test is the calcium score by electron beam computed tomography (EBCT). It needs to be interpreted according to age. It establishes plaque burden.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Adiponectin -- Clinical Significance -- Atherosclerosis
retrieved_docs.content: Serum adiponectin levels have also been shown to have an inverse relationship with intimal thickness, an important biomarker of atherosclerosis. At the cellular level, adiponectin has also demonstrated a role in slowing the transformation of macrophages to foam cells and consequently stalling progression to atherosclerosis in animal models. However, serum adiponectin does not yet enjoy acceptance as a risk marker for ASCVD prediction or management. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Intermediate Density Lipoprotein -- Clinical Significance -- Dysbetalipoproteinemia
retrieved_docs.content: The initial approach to treating type III hyperlipoproteinemia involves dietary and lifestyle modifications. Recommendations include reducing carbohydrate and fat intake, with an emphasis on replacing saturated fats with unsaturated fats. Obesity, diabetes mellitus, and metabolic syndrome can influence the clinical course, necessitating evaluation and management of these conditions. Combination therapy involving statins and fibrates is the treatment of choice if the lipid levels remain elevated despite lifestyle modification. [49] Indicators of therapeutic response include a reduction in non-HDL cholesterol levels and the resolution of palmar xanthomas.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Abdominal Aorta -- Clinical Significance -- Atherosclerosis
retrieved_docs.content: Atherosclerosis is a pathological process involving the deposition of fatty and fibrous plaques on the inner walls of the systemic circulatory vessels. The process can begin as early as childhood with the development of fatty streaks. In the United States, 1 in 6 teenagers was found to have abnormal intimal thickening on ultrasound. [32] Risk factors for atherosclerosis include hypertension, high levels of low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL), smoking, obesity, and diabetes mellitus. The pathophysiology involves endothelial cell dysfunction, dyslipidemia, inflammatory factors, and plaque rupture. Oxidized LDL is known to cause endothelial cell dysfunction leading to loss of nitric oxide-induced vasodilation. Macrophages that have taken up oxidized LDL release inflammatory cytokines and growth factors. [33] Atherosclerosis generally remains asymptomatic until luminal diameter reaches 70% to 80% of normal. Symptoms vary depending on the organ systems distal to the diseased vessels. Atherosclerosis of the aorta itself is generally asymptomatic due to its large caliber. However, marked stenosis of the coronary arteries is responsible for unstable angina and myocardial infarction. Atherosclerosis of the carotid arteries can result in cerebral ischemia, leading to symptoms of a stroke. Peripheral arterial atherosclerosis, also known as peripheral artery disease, can result in limb claudication, tingling, numbness, and non-healing ulcers. Atherosclerosis of the renal arteries often results in chronic kidney disease and renovascular hypertension. Preventative measures include diet, exercise, weight loss, and smoking cessation. Statins, blood pressure medications, and aspirin are the mainstays of treatment. Vascular bypass surgeries can re-establish flow in the limbs, brain, and heart.

retrieved_docs.content: The relationship between exercise and Lp(a) levels is still not fully understood. Some studies suggest that moderate exercise has little effect on serum Lp(a) concentration. However, other studies suggest that intense load-bearing exercise training, such as distance running or weight lifting, may increase serum Lp(a) levels over several months to years. [43]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2550
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary atherosclerosis and other heart disease is linked to inflammation of the pericardium (الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر). The symptom of chest constriction (خُنَاقٌ صَدْرِيّ) maps from coronary atherosclerosis and other heart disease, indicating that patients with this vascular condition may experience a sensation of tightness in the chest. In addition, inflammation of the pericardium (الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر) is clinically associated with an abnormal electrocardiogram (مخطط كهربية القلب غير طبيعي), reflecting that pericardial inflammation often produces characteristic ECG changes. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians correlate chest symptoms, underlying coronary pathology, pericardial inflammation, and ECG findings for more accurate diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة) and chest pain (ألم الصدر) are both clinically associated with hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم). This relationship indicates that elevated blood cholesterol levels can contribute to the development of acute coronary syndromes such as unstable angina and may manifest as chest discomfort. Recognizing hypercholesterolemia as an underlying factor for these cardiac presentations underscores the importance of lipid screening and management in patients who exhibit chest pain or anginal symptoms, thereby aiding in risk stratification and preventive cardiovascular care.

Overall, these interconnected concepts illustrate a cascade in which coronary atherosclerosis and its inflammatory morphology precede cardiac remodeling, are linked to chronic inflammation, produce detectable ECG changes, and may be mitigated by preserving cardiac muscle health, while also manifesting externally as skin‑visible vascular aging.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2814
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac disorder, coronary artery disease, hypercholesterolemia, and heart murmur are closely interrelated clinical entities. Cardiac disorder is clinically associated with both coronary artery disease and hypercholesterolemia. Coronary artery disease is clinically associated with hypercholesterolemia and also with the presence of a heart murmur. Hypercholesterolemia, in turn, is clinically associated with cardiac disorder. These connections underscore that elevated blood cholesterol contributes to the development of coronary artery disease and other cardiac disorders, and that coronary artery disease may manifest with a heart murmur, highlighting the importance of integrated assessment of lipid levels and cardiac function in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2908
retrieved_docs.content: Inflammatory morphology is recognized as an associated morphological pattern of coronary atherosclerosis and other heart disease, indicating that inflammatory changes in the arterial wall and cardiac tissue are characteristic features of these cardiovascular conditions. This relationship underscores the role of inflammation in the development and structural presentation of coronary atherosclerosis as well as a broader spectrum of heart diseases, highlighting its importance for diagnostic assessment, risk stratification, and therapeutic targeting in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1514
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary atherosclerosis and other heart disease is a cardiovascular condition that may present with findings in the perikard (pericardium). It is linked to الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر (pericarditis), indicating that inflammation of the pericardium can accompany or be related to the atherosclerotic process. The condition is also mapped from خُنَاقٌ صَدْرِيّ (chest constriction), suggesting that patients often describe a constrictive sensation in the chest as a symptom of the underlying disease. Moreover, الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر is clinically associated with مخطط كهربية القلب غير طبيعي (an abnormal electrocardiogram), highlighting that pericarditis frequently produces ECG abnormalities that can aid in diagnosis. Recognizing these interconnections—pericardial involvement, chest‑constriction symptoms, and ECG changes—can improve clinical assessment and management of patients with coronary atherosclerosis and related heart disease.

These entities are organized hierarchically: general cardiac and vascular diagnoses and risk factors feed into specific diagnostic categories; diagnostic categories give rise to biopsy techniques; biopsy techniques serve as parents for detailed ventricular or pericardial biopsy procedures; biopsies and excisions connect to broader operative groups such as “HEART: GENERAL AND MISCELLANEOUS OPERATIVE PROCEDURES” and “other or heart procedures.” This integrated framework delineates the relationships among anatomical structures (heart, pericardium, papillary muscles), diagnostic modalities, therapeutic interventions, and related disease conditions, supporting comprehensive cardiac and vascular patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1746
retrieved_docs.content: Alirocumab, a PCSK9‑inhibiting medication used to lower LDL‑cholesterol, may have its effects mitigated by the process of احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ (cardiac muscle development). In clinical observations, احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ is also associated with مرض النقرس (gout), indicating that patients with gout may exhibit alterations in cardiac muscle formation that could influence the therapeutic response to alirocumab. Understanding the interplay between alirocumab, cardiac muscle development, and gout is important for optimizing lipid‑lowering strategies and managing cardiovascular risk in individuals affected by these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2832
retrieved_docs.content: The cardiac disorder (اضطراب قلبي) is clinically associated with coronary artery disease (تصلب الشريان التاجي), indicating that patients with coronary atherosclerosis frequently exhibit cardiac abnormalities and that the presence of a cardiac disorder may suggest underlying coronary artery disease, a relationship that is important for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2462
retrieved_docs.content: HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid modifying drugs constitute a therapeutic class of statin medications that lower cholesterol by inhibiting the HMG‑CoA reductase enzyme in the hepatic cholesterol synthesis pathway. Atorvastatin, fluvastatin, and pravastatin are individual agents that belong to this class. By reducing low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and modestly increasing high‑density lipoprotein (HDL), these drugs are employed to manage hyperlipidemia and to lower the risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Their shared mechanism of action and lipid‑modifying effects make them central components of preventive cardiology and patient care strategies aimed at controlling dyslipidemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2830
retrieved_docs.content: Chest pain (ألم الصَدْر) is clinically associated with hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم). Elevated blood cholesterol promotes atherosclerotic plaque development in coronary arteries, which can impair myocardial blood flow and present as chest pain. Recognizing this link enables clinicians to assess patients with chest pain for underlying lipid abnormalities and to consider lipid‑lowering interventions as part of comprehensive cardiovascular risk management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 581
retrieved_docs.content: احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ is clinically associated with both الراجفان الأذيني and مرض النقرس, indicating that the development or remodeling of cardiac muscle may increase the risk of atrial fibrillation and gout. In addition, احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ may prevent the use or effectiveness of alirocumab, a PCSK9‑inhibiting lipid‑lowering agent. Because الراجفان الأذيني is a contraindication for both drospirenone and estetrol monohydrate, these hormonal agents should be avoided in patients with atrial fibrillation. Recognizing the link between cardiac muscle changes, atrial fibrillation, and gout, while selecting appropriate lipid‑lowering and hormonal therapies, is essential for optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1647
retrieved_docs.content: التصلب العصيدي is linked to ذَبْهَةُ برنزميتال, indicating a possible association between this vascular hardening disorder and that specific condition. Clinically, التصلب العصيدي is associated with several observable findings: the presence of ال​نفخة الانقباضية (a systolic murmur), لغط الشريان السباتي (a carotid bruit), and the need for حفظ الصحة الروتيني (routine health maintenance) to monitor and manage disease progression. In addition, التصلب العصيدي has a recognized subtype, التصلب العصيدي المعمم, which represents a more generalized form of the disease. Pharmacologically, the PCSK9 inhibitor alirocumab may prevent the development or progression of التصلب العصيدي, suggesting a therapeutic role for lipid‑lowering strategies in patients at risk for this condition. Together, these relationships highlight the clinical manifestations, related conditions, disease subtypes, and potential preventive treatment options relevant to the management of التصلب العصيدي.
